checks.label: Chat route exposes async completion enqueue path

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/roadmap-signals.md contains 'The primary chat loop is queue-coupled.'
checks.label: Architecture docs still flag chat-loop dependency coupling

manual_prompts: does provider-output validation reliably reject malformed responses before persistence?
can worker crashes or restarts leave threads locked or duplicate turns in edge cases?
is degraded behavior for Redis outage explicit to operators and end users?
domains.name: Core Loop Integrity
domains.summary: Chat route, worker, queue, task-event, and completion-pipeline anchors are all present. The repo also explicitly records Redis and worker coupling as an operational risk, so restart and degraded-mode behavior still need human validation.

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/data-and-storage.md matches 'Key Entities and Collections', 'Hard invariants'
checks.label: Data and storage docs enumerate entities and invariants
Modules docs describe subsystem ownership seams
Repo-local docs warn about contract drift in tool primitives

manual_prompts: are lifecycle transitions for threads, documents, jobs, and events stable across migrations and releases?
do schema changes preserve backward compatibility for existing API and worker flows?
are ownership and identity constraints enforced consistently in code, not just described in docs?
domains.name: Primitive Stability
domains.summary: The repo has a central models file plus current docs for entities, invariants, subsystem seams, and ownership guidance. Contract-drift warnings around legacy tools versus the command bus suggest at least one primitive family is still in transition.

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/system-overview.md did not match expected text: 'command bus', 'Cron and job execution', 'Coding results now return through Guardian'
checks.label: Architecture docs describe command bus, cron, and coding-agent seams

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/modules-and-ownership.md references 'legacy tools shim'; route is absent and should remain compatibility-only if reintroduced
checks.label: Legacy /tools compatibility route status

manual_prompts: does the extension boundary allow new workflows without kernel edits?
can new automation paths continue to prefer durable command-bus and cron lanes over ad hoc process-local behavior?
do Guardian intent-spine and coding-agent return-path seams stay policy-governed across chat, automation, and future plugin entrypoints?
is self-extending plugin governance explicit enough to prevent capability drift at install and runtime binding boundaries?
domains.name: Extension Boundary
domains.summary: Command bus, cron execution, Guardian intent-orchestration seams, and the Guardian-mediated coding result path provide the current extension boundary evidence. This remains a cautionary domain because extension governance and cross-surface maturity still require manual review.

checks.label: Config and ops docs enumerate health and metrics surfaces
Sync API exposes a health endpoint

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/config-and-ops.md contains 'Structured JSON logger setup is `Unverified`'
checks.label: Observability docs leave some logging guarantees unverified

manual_prompts: do logs include enough request, task, and identity context to debug failures without reproducing them locally?
are queue depth, worker liveness, and dependency health observable enough for operational triage?
do restart scenarios preserve enough traceability across task events, outbox events, and sync subscribers?
domains.name: Observability
domains.summary: Repo-local evidence shows explicit health endpoints, task-event streaming, metrics, and sync health reporting. The docs still label parts of the logging and event-delivery story as unverified or restart-sensitive, so incident-debug depth needs manual confirmation.

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/data-and-storage.md matches 'Postgres is the source of truth', 'queue-backed'
checks.label: Data docs define Postgres as source of truth and queue-backed execution
Models include durable run and idempotency anchors
Sync API acknowledges idempotent event ingestion
Roadmap docs warn that sync delivery is not yet durable

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/tech-debt-and-risks.md contains 'Redis in Compose is configured without durable persistence'
checks.label: Risk register warns about Redis persistence or replay gaps

manual_prompts: are degraded modes explicit for Redis outage?
can failed chat, ingestion, and cron work be replayed without duplicate side effects?
are restart, retry, and dead-letter expectations documented strongly enough for operators to recover safely?
domains.name: Durability & Recovery
domains.summary: The repo contains durable Postgres models, queue-backed execution, and explicit idempotency anchors for some run types. At the same time, repo-local docs warn about non-durable sync delivery, Redis persistence limits in Compose, and incomplete replay ergonomics.

checks.label: System overview documents multiple client surfaces
System overview describes a frontend API/runtime layer
Config and ops docs record desktop/runtime overrides

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/roadmap-signals.md contains 'Frontend routing and shell orchestration are mostly hand-rolled'
checks.label: Repo-local docs still describe shell-level coupling

manual_prompts: do non-web clients use the same auth, event, and workflow contracts as the web UI without special-case backend logic?
can CLI, voice, or agent surfaces complete the core loop without relying on browser-only state assumptions?
are surface-specific UX choices cleanly separated from core service behavior?
domains.name: Alternate Surface Readiness
domains.summary: Codexify clearly serves at least web and desktop surfaces through shared backend APIs, and the docs describe runtime overrides for those clients. The scanned evidence does not prove that headless, mobile, or automation clients are first-class enough to justify a machine-suggested score without human review.

checks.label: Federation route includes trust-policy and version anchors
Architecture docs describe federation and sync flow
Roadmap docs warn that sync subscriptions are process-local

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/tech-debt-and-risks.md contains 'Federation is security- and config-sensitive'
checks.label: Risk register warns that federation remains security- and config-sensitive

manual_prompts: can peers verify identity, rotation, revocation, and trust-policy changes safely over time?
are conflict resolution and replay semantics strong enough under partitions or restart gaps?
are degraded federation modes explicit when relays, trust policy, or egress checks fail?
domains.name: Federation Readiness
domains.summary: Federation routes, trust-policy checks, sync endpoints, and version-aware diff handling exist in the repo. The same repo-local evidence also says sync delivery is process-local and federation is still a high-blast-radius, config-sensitive area, so the likely band remains low.

checks.label: Tech debt and risks documentation present
Data docs describe hard invariants and explicit access boundaries

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/system-overview.md matches 'enforcement is in route/auth/policy code, not in prompts'
checks.label: System overview states that enforcement lives in code and policy layers

checks.evidence: docs/architecture/modules-and-ownership.md contains 'This repo does not declare formal team ownership in code'
checks.label: Ownership authority is still informal in the scanned docs

manual_prompts: are governance invariants documented and enforced?
is extension authority explicit enough to prevent accidental policy or boundary regressions?
are versioning, migration, and compatibility expectations strong enough to support rolling architectural change?
domains.name: Governance Readiness
domains.summary: The repo has a current ownership map, risk register, roadmap signal doc, identity policy, and documented hard invariants. Those are solid governance inputs, but the scanned evidence still leaves extension authority and enforcement coverage too cross-cutting for a truthful machine score.

No internal-only or quarantined surface is part of the release claim.
